old_claim.note: Notarial Records provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct edge remains because the charterparty node is still a broad placeholder-dated shipping-contract concept, while notarial_records is now scoped to source-backed Genoese notarial registers dated 1155.

source_shape.source_locator: Cambridge dates Giovanni Scriba's oldest surviving European notarial cartularies to 1155-64; the source does not support them as a prerequisite for a generic year-500 charterparty node.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source supports twelfth-century Genoese notarial cartularies, not a year-500 dependency for all charterparty shipping contracts.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Keeping the edge would make a 1155 source-backed register tradition a prerequisite for an earlier, broader shipping-contract placeholder.

ontology_before: Charterparty shipping contracts depended directly on notarial_records through a generic expert-inference edge.
ontology_after: Charterparty shipping contracts no longer depend directly on medieval notarial registers until that node is itself source-checked and dated compatibly.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: charterparty_shipping_contracts no longer has a direct notarial_records prerequisite.
would_reject_if: Charterparty shipping contracts were resourced and redated to a notarial-register-based twelfth-century Mediterranean scope.
A source showed the earliest scoped charterparties required Genoese notarial registers.
The notarial_records node were split into an earlier generic legal-scribe record node.
short_reason: A 1155 Genoese notarial-register node cannot be a direct prerequisite for a broad year-500 charterparty placeholder.
